speaker: interviewer
text: 18Th, 1995. Ruth, we left off on the last tape talking about your father losing a great deal of his business and having to sell the business, and you were with your mother in an apartment. Where was your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister left in 1936 to go to Israel. She had a boyfriend. She was almost five years older than I am. And he wrote to her. He wanted to marry her. Please come to Israel. He was from a city called Isen in the Rhineland. But he worked in Nuremberg, and they met. And he had the chance to go to Israel-- Palestine. And after a while, it was decided that because he shouldn't come back, because the minute he would set foot into Germany he would be arrested, that she would try to get a visa, and he got her a visa to go to Israel. And in '36, we celebrated her engagement with him there. And we were sitting there with a picture of him on the table, and his mother came to visit. And it was very sad because two days later, she left for Palestine. And she took along a wedding dress, and she couldn't take very much as a family. They wouldn't let her take much. She took a couple of suitcases with clothes. That's all she took. And she was married in Tel Aviv. Some friends of her future husband made a little wedding for them, and we had probably figured out that we would never see her again, and it was very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- do you remember what you might have said to one another before she left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. Some of those things you don't forget. We would play music from Edvard Grieg on the stone mountain. We had a record, and we played that. And somehow, it broke. And the day after she left, my mother gave me a package, and she said your sister bought you this as a present. It was the new record from Grieg. And of course, we cried like a baby. Mail was not too good in those days. Now you have air letters. But it was very difficult to tear the family apart. My father was still in Furth at that time, but when the doorbell rang, you never knew who was there, and there was no escape from this apartment house that we lived in. There was one exit, and there was another exit that went into a place in the back where you cleaned your carpets twice a year. But if the Nazis would have come, you just couldn't escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was this like for you as a child, living under these kind of conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't a child anymore. You grew up fast. I was 15 then when she left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It sort of broke my heart, but I at that time said I would never leave my parents because I probably could have gone to Palestine too because I belonged to these Jewish organizations. I forget what they're called. Haganah? No, it was something else. But we would meet, and we would talk about Palestine and what we would do if we would go there. And of course, I wasn't in school anymore, so my Gentile friends didn't talk to me anymore. Nobody called. And the Gentile people that lived in the apartment house, they would spit at you when they came down, you walked up. So we said this is no good. We have to go to America. My father met a young man when he was in the army, and this young man-- this is a different story, but it belongs-- had emigrated to the United States in 1913. And in 1914, when the war broke out, he was in Germany visiting his parents. And the Germans wouldn't let him out anymore because he was not an American citizen yet, so he had to stay in Germany and fight on the German side, and that's where he met my father. And both of them got through the war OK. And they became lifelong friends, and they kept writing to each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in 1933, he wrote to my father, come. I'll get you an affidavit. And my father wrote him back, I'm not leaving. I'm going to be the backup light. And in 1933, one didn't consider leaving, which of course was stupid, but who knows the future? And then, by 1936, he wrote and he said, I'm trying to get you an affidavit anyway. Try to get a number because America only let in 2,700 people or something like that a year. And finally, the number we got was 15,422.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother had a brother in New York who was shipped there by my grandparents as a young boy, and she wrote to him, and she said, try to get us some affidavits because it's much better to get affidavits from a relative, from family. And they sent us affidavits. We had those. They came, but there was no money in the background. They had no money. They were working people. But it still-- because you had to say that you were able to support yourself when you got here. And then, it came to the point that it was just impossible to get to America because your number wasn't called.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, going back to the other thing, my father went to Hamburg to tell these people and to show them how to set it up, and it took months, and months, and months. And one day-- and he would always go to the same restaurant up there to eat. One day, we got a phone call saying, your man left. No names. We don't know where he is. And they hung up. So we didn't know. My mother and I are sitting in Furth. Was he arrested? Was he in the concentration camp? Where was he? Two weeks-- nothing, absolutely nothing. And we always said, well, if he is in a concentration camp, they would have come. They would have told us. Nobody came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day we got a postcard stamped from Holland, the Netherlands. Having a wonderful time. Wish you were here, Julius-- Julia. He had escaped to the Netherlands. He drove his car to the Dutch border, and the Nazis were following him and shooting at him. And because of that, he didn't go to visit his parents who lived right near there. It was on his 50th birthday, which is on the 27th of November, which is ice cold in Europe-- snow and ice. And at one point, they got so close that he left the car, and he started running. And he fell into a ditch with water, and he was freezing. He had nothing on him except his gold watch and a pair of socks. Why he had a pair of socks in his pocket, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as he got on the road, he saw a man with a horse cart. And he stopped him, and he said, am I on my way to Amsterdam? And the man answered him in Dutch. And he explained to him what had happened, and the man explained to him that he will drive him with his horse cart into Amsterdam, which was hours away. And he drove him to the railroad station, where there was this help. We have it in some stations and airports too when people are lost in something. That's where he deposited him. And my father took off his gold watch and gave it to him. And it happened on the 27th of November. What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938. It was 1938. So of course, we were elated, but we didn't let anybody know so it should come back, and they would come for us. It was one of the happier times during the Hitler time. Food was rationed, of course. You didn't have that much food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working. I was working for this dentist, and one of the patients was a very young, nice young man, and he introduced himself. His name was George Rau. And he asked me, would I go with him to have a cup of coffee? Because you didn't go out like you did here. And the dentist is standing back. You know, he's like my father, and he's going like this, yes, do this. I know the family, that kind of thing. So we made a date. And he took me to a coffee house, and we had coffee and cake. And from then on, we saw each other every single day until the day he was arrested. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Nazis brought out this law in 1935 that Jews were not allowed to associate with males or females of the Aryan sect that-- the Germans were all Aryans, and the Jews were nothing. They were dirt. So lots of Jewish men sometimes had Gentile friends and girlfriends. Not vice versa so much, I don't think. But he did have a German girlfriend, but she married somebody else, and he had given her up completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we met every single day. And for the two years that we met every day, he brought a present every single day. And my mother always was saying, what do you want with the old man? He's much too old for you. But I was 17, and I was very much in love. How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: 33, 16 Years older. And we would meet every day, and then on weekends we would go out into the country. He had a car. And we'd go picnicking with other friends sometimes, sometimes alone. And his family had a hop factory near a city called Wurzburg, which is known. This was in Ochsenfurt, and it was on the Main River. And we would go there on weekends, couple other-- two other people, and we spent the weekend. And there was a maid, and she would cook. And we would go swimming in the Main River, and we had a wonderful time. And Mondays, of course, we had to come back and go to work and hope that everything would be all right. He would spend six weeks there, and then the next six weeks, a cousin of the family would go there. And there were three of them, and they took turns. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had planned-- he was very peculiar in the way that he-- he, of course, was trying to go to America too by that time. Everybody was trying to come to America. In America, you can't buy things, and everything he bought had to be just right because we can't replace it in America the way-- you don't know how to picture this country. You've never been in this country, and it's so far away. It's over an ocean. There weren't any airplanes in those days that you could hop. So we would go shopping sometimes and buy supplies, and he would pack them and put them away when we--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was also trying to get his affidavit. And one day, he said, I have to go to Switzerland. And I said, you're not coming back. He says, I'm coming back. I said, you'd be foolish if you come back. Then he went to Switzerland, and I found that out later. He took money to Switzerland and deposited it in Switzerland. He came back. And I said as much as I love you, and as much as I'd like to be with you, I think you're crazy. Why did you come back? He says, I can't leave my parents. I got to take care of my parents, and we want to go to America together. I had to come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you could have waited for me in Switzerland. I could have made it somehow out of the country. Of course, you never knew because one day I was-- Friday nights, I always bought flowers for my mother. With as little money as I had, I took some money and bought flowers for Shabbos. And I walked into the flower shop, and one of the brown shirts followed me and wanted to pick me up. He didn't know, of course, I was Jewish. You didn't wear stars then yet. And I didn't know how to get away from him, and the woman in the flower shop told me-- [COUGHING] excuse me. Go out the back door.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I slipped out the back door, and I had to run three blocks around to come back to my apartment house. But it was frightening because if he would have picked me up, and if he would have known, that would have been the end of me. This was in 1938. All this was in 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had been seeing each other already for a year. In summers, we would spend out in the country when we had days off. And in the winter-- we would never go to a movie or to a concert together. Jews weren't allowed to go to these places. And when we wanted to hear music, I sometimes snuck into a church to hear the organ music. I was so starved for music. And then, sometimes friends would get together and play quartet piano and violin. Somebody would sing. You'd try to lead a fairly normal life. We had friends, and one after the other, they vanished. One of them was sent to Dachau. His name was Hans Rinskopf. And six months later, all of a sudden, he's back. They let him out. They had beaten him so unmercifully that both his hip bones were broken, and he had to walk with two canes. And he swung his legs out like in a half-circle, first one, and then the other, and that's the way he walked. It was unbearable. And he went to the Jewish hospital where this Dr. Frank was, and they couldn't help him. There was nothing they could do for him. So we went to his house all the time, and we played cards with him. You had to try to keep your sanity. It was difficult, but we would read books together or make dinner for one another. Everybody brought something because you didn't have much. And what happened one day-- we're coming, but he was gone. They had picked him up again, without his crutches, without his canes, and we never heard from him again. They had shipped him to Dachau. That, they told us, told his parents. But nobody ever saw him again. In the meantime, my uncle, my father's only remaining brother, was arrested and was shipped to Dachau. And the tortures he had to go through were indescribable, like putting a hose down his throat and turning on the water and standing out in the cold without shoes and socks. They took away the clothes, of course, and they were wearing those pajama-type things, all in the freezing cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was all interrupted by Crystal Night. My mother and I and this old man that lived with us were in the apartment, and I got up in the morning to go to work. And I get on a streetcar, and I see people moving away from me. And I see a truck going by the streetcar and men standing in them. I said, what in the hell is this? Where are they going in a truck? I said, oh, my God, they have beards and hats on. They're Jews, truckloads full of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had arrested all the men and women and brought them to a place called the [NON-ENGLISH], where operas would be played and music would be played, and lectures-- and they made the-- they brought out scissors, and they cut off the beards of the religious people who wore beards because of religion, not because they liked it. And they made them scrub the floors with toothbrushes, and they made them line up and take their pants down to show that they were Jews, that they were circumcised. It's not like now, where everybody gets-- and somehow, they missed my mother and me. And I found this other stuff out much later.

speaker: interviewee
text: I get to Nuremberg to my dentist's office, and it was fine. But across the hall was a supply house-- office-- a dental supply, and the people that owned it-- the name was Reisner. And I walked in there, and I saw all the drawers were pulled out. They had these narrow drawers filled with false teeth, thousands and thousands turned upside down, everything poured into a heap, all mirrors broken, all the chairs broken, everything turned upside down. And where were the Reisners? Nowhere in sight. I said, what happened here? Ordinary thief doesn't do that. So I get back into the dentist's office, and he is not showing up. And the other assistant, who was Gentile, didn't show up either. So I called him at his home, and he says, where are you? I said, I'm in the office. How come you're not here? And he says, everybody's been arrested, but I think I wasn't arrested because I used to be an officer in the army. Oh, yeah? They forgot him too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I told him what had happened to the Reisners, but the Reisners were arrested. They arrested them out of the house and put them into Dachau. It had seemed that from Nuremberg and Furth, everybody went to Dachau first before they were shipped someplace else. And so he said, I don't want you to stay there. I want you to go home. By the time I got home, of course, my mother had heard about it too, and everybody else in the house-- there was another Jewish family. They were arrested. It was the most frightening thing. You couldn't go out. You couldn't go buy food. You didn't have refrigerators that you had food on hand. You went marketing every day or every second day. Besides, you didn't have the money to do it. The phone was disconnected. There was no phone to get in touch with anybody. I couldn't find out if my friend had been arrested or not. The people that lived on the same floor with us, the Gentile people, were halfway decent. They got a phone call saying, from George, I'm home. He was home, says that we got to get out of here. We all got to get out of here. Some of the people that were arrested that Crystal Night came back. Some of them didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: The dentist told me, don't come to work. See what you can do where you can help. So I called up the Jewish hospital, and I said, do you need help? Yes. So I went there, and I helped there because first, all the nurses left, and then they had some sisters. They stayed. But because there wasn't enough help and nobody was cleaning or doing anything-- after all, they weren't sisters of mercy. They were Gentile sisters of mercy. They turned around, and they left. And here were a skeleton staff of Jewish people who had no idea about taking care of sick people or people that were hurt, like for instance the guy who was hit in the head with a board that had a nail sticking out. And this Dr. Frank operated on him, and we assisted. And the man lived. And then when he was alive, he says, what did you keep me alive for? So I'll get shipped to a concentration camp? Some of them I remember. Some of them-- not many. Lots of them died. They were beaten so badly and-- broken bones. At this point, when people were arrested, was it only the men that were going to the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they arrest the women and children also?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they let them go home, yeah. They were all there, except our house. That was-- they missed that, where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent the women and children back home, but the men were sent to camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: To camps, yes, and some of the women never saw their husbands again, or brothers. They never came back. It was very sad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] By that time, then, the fright and the despair in the Jewish population was so deep and so strong that some of them committed suicide. There were some. And then, the rabbis pleaded. It's against God's law. No matter what happens, you've got to stay alive. You've got to try for what comes later. There must be an end to this. Nobody, nobody imagined what was going to come later. It was just impossible. The Jewish butcher shop had to close. All the stores that were owned by Jews-- the Jews were thrown out, and the SS and SR-- it was given to them. The stores that were established were given to them. People who had died-- the stores were just left, so they took some of the SS and SR and said, you're out of the SS. I'll give you this store. These were well-established firms. And Jews were not allowed to have anything anymore. Bank accounts were confiscated. Then came the decree that you had to turn in all your jewelry, all your silver, any gold. They had stations where you had to bring it. They didn't come pick it up. You had to bring it to them. And my mother, who was a very feisty lady, said I'm not going to give all of it away. I'm  not just going to do it. I'm going to give some of it as a token, and she took her silver service for 12, and she sold it to somebody on a pittance, just a pittance. And then, we got a notice that we had to leave our apartment. They had lived there since 1914, and from one day to the next, we had to give up the apartment. And she found somebody who bought the piano, my father's piano. And when that was carried out of the apartment, it was like a casket was being carried out. I mean, these were material things, but still, it showed the disruption of the family. It showed a disruption of our life. It showed the disruption of something we had taken for granted. It was just no more.
